context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on neoclassical vs dependency theories of economic development. I’m kinda unsure how to answer the planning part and the WTO/policy space link.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the main moving parts. Let's start at the beginning: what do you already know about what neoclassical economists say drives economic growth? Don't worry about being precise — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the key tensions in the prompt but hasn't yet demonstrated what they already know. Before scaffolding, I should probe their existing understanding of neoclassical theory first, since that's the logical starting point of the prompt. Keep it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: uh, I think it’s stuff like markets, investment, trade, and maybe letting prices work? idk if they mostly see the state as getting in the way or if that’s too simplified.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point — markets, prices, trade and investment are indeed central. Now push that a bit further: if markets are seen as the efficient allocators of resources, what does that imply about whether a state *needs* to step in and plan? What role, if any, would planning have in that worldview?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about neoclassical economics. I should affirm what's right and push them to think more carefully about the role of the state/planning specifically, since that's what the prompt asks. I'll ask a pointed Socratic question to move them toward the planning dimension without giving it away.

exchanges.student: my first guess is planning isn’t super necessary there, since markets are supposed to allocate efficiently. maybe the state just sets basic rules/institutions, but not heavy direction?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now flip it: what do you know about dependency theory's view of why developing countries stay poor? And from that, would you expect dependency theorists to *want* more planning, or less?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and arriving at the right intuition. I should affirm this and push them to contrast it with dependency theory, which is the next logical step in the prompt. This will help them build toward the planning comparison the prompt requires.

exchanges.student: i think dependency says poorer countries get stuck b/c the global system benefits richer “core” countries? so tbh I’d expect more planning, not less, but I’m not fully sure why.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. So if the global system is the problem, what might planning *do* to address that? Think about what tools a state might use to protect or develop its own economy rather than just plugging into global markets.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right instinct about dependency theory favoring more planning. I should push them to articulate *why* - connecting the diagnosis (exploitation by the core) to the prescription (what planning could do about it). This will strengthen their argument in the reflection piece.

exchanges.student: maybe tariffs, industrial policy, controlling foreign investment, or protecting local industries for a while? I’m guessing planning would be a way to stop being pushed around by global markets.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — those tools are at the heart of it. Now here's the bridge to the last part of the prompt: if dependency-style thinking favors those tools, what do you think happens when international institutions like the WTO set rules that *limit* a country's ability to use tariffs or industrial policy? Who does that remind you of?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building good intuitions connecting dependency theory to state-led development tools. Now I should bridge them toward the third part of the prompt about WTO/policy space, which is the most complex part. A Socratic question linking their current reasoning to contemporary global governance will help them make that connection themselves.

exchanges.student: ohh ok, that sounds like the “policy space” issue rn. it kinda echoes dependency theory bc poorer countries might say global rules lock them into weaker positions while richer countries already developed using more protection.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really strong connection — and it's essentially the core argument you need to make in your paper. You've now touched on all four parts of the prompt. Before you start writing, can you tell me in one or two sentences what the through-line of your reflection would be? What's the main argument connecting all these pieces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong and correct connection between dependency theory and the policy space argument. This is a key insight for their paper. Since we've now covered the main conceptual terrain of the prompt across several exchanges, I should affirm this and push them to start thinking about how to structure their reflection piece, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe my main point is that neoclassical theory treats development as coming mainly from efficient markets, so planning has a limited role, while dependency theory sees underdevelopment as produced by unequal global structures, so planning becomes more necessary. and current debates over WTO rules and policy space seem like a modern version of that dependency critique.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-formed argument — it's clear, covers all the prompt's questions, and makes a genuine claim. Now the real work begins: get a draft going. As you write, think about whether you have specific examples or thinkers to ground each part, since a reflection at this level benefits from concrete references. What do you have in your readings that could support this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong and coherent through-line that addresses all four parts of the prompt. This is a good moment to push them toward drafting rather than continuing to discuss concepts, since we've covered the main terrain. I should affirm the quality of their thinking and redirect them to writing.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m blanking on the specific thinkers rn. can u help me remember which names usually fit neoclassical vs dependency so I can anchor it?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth figuring out yourself — go back to your course readings and syllabus. What authors or texts were assigned for the weeks covering these theories? Even a quick scan of your reading list should surface some names.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide names directly, which would be giving them part of the answer. Instead, I should prompt them to think about what they've read in the course rather than supplying the information myself. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, I’ll check that. but while I do, can u tell me if Rostow counts here or if that’s more modernization than neoclassical?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to wrestle with. What do you know about Rostow's core argument? And how would you describe the difference between modernization theory and neoclassical economics as you understand them so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a direct classification question about Rostow. Rather than answering, I should turn it back to them Socratically - asking what they know about Rostow's argument and how they'd define the boundary between modernization and neoclassical theory. This keeps them doing the intellectual work and is a useful conceptual distinction for their paper.

exchanges.student: I think Rostow is more stage-based, like countries move through steps to “takeoff,” so that feels more modernization than straight neoclassical econ. I’m a bit stuck on how cleanly I need to separate those in my reflection.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about Rostow is reasonable. But ask yourself: does the prompt specifically require you to separate modernization from neoclassical theory, or is the more important task to get your argument on paper? You have a strong through-line already — I'd suggest starting your draft now and making those finer distinctions as they become relevant. What's stopping you from writing a first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about Rostow and is now questioning how much precision is needed in their paper. This is a mild detour - the prompt doesn't specifically ask them to distinguish modernization from neoclassical theory. I should validate their reasoning briefly and redirect them toward drafting, since we've been in discussion long enough and they have the conceptual tools they need.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student to generate their own ideas rather than providing answers. No near-submission-ready content was given, key ideas were student-led, and the tutor appropriately redirected when the student asked for direct answers (e.g., Turn 8 on thinkers). Scaffolding was well-executed, building progressively from neoclassical basics to dependency theory to the WTO/policy space connection. Responses were concise and appropriately sized. No significant deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content; all key reasoning steps were elicited from the student through questions. In Turn 6, the tutor affirmed the student's connection but did not state it first. In Turn 8, the tutor explicitly refused to supply thinker names and redirected to course readings. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations found. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., 'That's a solid starting point,' 'You're on the right track') and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback in Turn 7 ('think about whether you have specific examples or thinkers to ground each part') and in Turn 10 redirected the student toward drafting with a methodological rationale. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred; the conversation advanced steadily through all four prompt components. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise, interactive, and supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
